speaker: interviewer
text: Born at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was the first night I ever slept by myself alone. Never before. It was so scary that when I woke up, I thought I'm going to go mad. I really, I thought I'm not going to make it. Because I was so bewildered. I realized that I don't know-- I have no plan. I have no plan. Where am I going to go? I have no permit. Who am I going to ask for food now? I was so scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: The worst was the scariness. The scariness that somebody is going to give me away. And this was only the first day. And I had to live several years like this. Can you imagine? It was the very first day and I wasn't prepared. Nobody prepared me what I was have to do. My grandmother only said I have to survive, but I didn't know how.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I started to walk out of-- you know, first of all I have to tell you. You see, there were roosters. The farmer had roosters, you know, every-- many farmers had roosters. And when the roosters start 'cocorico!' you know, and he woke me up like an alarm clock. The roosters woke me up and that's when I woke up. And when I woke up I realized I'm all alone. It was scary. And I was so scared that I didn't know how to talk even to a person that I never talked before. Always my grandmother talked this way. And I didn't know how to face anybody, how to speak up. I didn't know how to speak. I knew what I must do, that I have to survive, but I didn't know how or how to speak to anybody. I never talked to anybody by myself. And I didn't know how to face this man. I didn't know what to say. I didn't know. I was totally bewildered. And instead of talking to the man and saying something, please hide me or whatever, give me some, I didn't say nothing. When those roosters woke me up, the first thing that came, I have to get out of here. I didn't know. Just like my grandmother would wake up at the dawn and she would lead us out someplace, that's what I did. But I did the wrong thing. Instead of going away from the village, I started to walk towards the village. It shows you how unprepared I was. I started to walk to almost to where my grandmother lived. And she told me something that if something happens to me, go to this lady. I forgot her name. I know I wrote it down somewhere, but right now I cannot remember her name. She had a tartak. They were the only people in the village that had a tartak.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a tartak? Tartak is a lumber yard where they cut wood. And wood was very important in that village because houses were built. They were used for heating, for warming, for heating. So wood was very-- and they cut wood. They took logs, they cut them into small pieces and they sold this. This was called tartak.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lady of the tartak, they had two sons and her husband, and they lived almost opposite across the meadow from my grandparents. So she told, my grandmother told me, says, if something happen, go to her. Because I gave her, I'm going to use the words my grandmother used, [NON-ENGLISH], which means a fortune. She must have given her something that was worth a fortune. Now, what it is, I don't remember. And she says, if something happens, go to her maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. I was walking to her house. And I walked into her house and she-- when she saw me. She was scared because a Jew was not allowed to be seen there. She could have been-- she says. You know, it was true that if somebody, another Pole saw me and they knew I was Jewish, they could have been killed. So I walked in and she says go up to my attic. Stay there meanwhile. And, you know, in the attic, there was just a tiny little window. And there was some straw there or something, I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was empty and all alone. Imagine a nine and a half year old child sitting there whole day or a few days. I was so lonely and frightened. And I didn't know what was going to be with me. I was frightened, terrible sad. It was such sadness in me. Well you know, to put a child all alone, you know, without anybody to talk to, so lonely. And everything, all the bad things, I thought that was going to be the end of me. And I didn't know yet what happened to my grandmother, to my sister. And she said just stay there. She was like indirectly maybe she was trying to see if she can hear some news what happened. Because news got around very quickly. They caught this Jew, that Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't remember, I can't remember whether it was a day or two or-- I don't think it was a full week. I don't remember. It could have been two days. And she found out my grandmother was killed with my sister. After that night-- I don't know that night what happened. But if I wouldn't have gotten away I probably would have been killed, which day I don't know. But she said my grandmother was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know exactly how this happened, but only when I went back to Poland did I find out a little bit more. Only so many years later I find out a little bit more what happened. I never asked, because I knew it was terrible. I didn't even try to think about it. Because I knew it was a terrible thing to be killed. Every child of nine understand what it means to be killed. They were killed, shot. Later I found out killed by a gun, thrown into a grave in the cemetery like you throw in an animal. And that was the end of my grandmother with my sister. Till today they're laying somewhere, their bones in that grave without a marker, without anything. And I had to go on with my life. I had to go on and save my life. And the woman, after when she found out that my grandmother was killed, she no longer had to be afraid. She says, I don't want, I can't keep you. See, as long as she thought my grandma was alive she figured, you know, she will maybe keep me. But then when she found out, she didn't want to. She said, I cannot keep you here. And she said go to the end. I think she gave me the idea to go to the end of the village where there was like, there were scattered houses and there was like woods. And I went in that direction. And how I found this particular woman I don't remember. That's the block. I have a total block. But that woman was the one that helped me the most. I slept sometimes on the ground, on the ground, many a night. I don't remember how many nights or how. But I remember many, I was sleeping, not permanently. But I would wander here, wander there. But many a night I slept and she gave me sometimes food. And as she had very little, because she was from the poorest family. She didn't even have a wooden floor in her house. One room, she had. And they didn't even have room to sleep, all four of the family. They had to climb up on a ladder and sleep on the-- like, I was never even up on the ladder. I didn't have the nerve to go up there and see under what condition. But I understand they had probably what to cover themselves. Maybe there was straw, straw mattress. There were enough room for all four of them. But there was not even enough room downstairs, because I remember clearly the room. There was one bed in it, like a big bed. And they couldn't sleep three women, two daughters, and a mother, and a son. The husband wasn't around. I don't know what happened, whether he died or whatever. I didn't ask questions in those days too much. I didn't know how to talk too much either because I was told to be quiet, not to annoy people. I was unobtrusive and I tried to be. If I didn't get food, I didn't need eat. If they gave me, I ate. If they didn't I-- was so good that you wouldn't believe it how good I was. I wasn't, you know, pushy. I was very, you know. And I tried to survive. All I knew that I don't care if I don't eat, I do eat, or be cold, but I knew I must, one thing I have to do, I must survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was it from the time that you left that tartak till you found that woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't very long. Was a few days. It was within that week, I believe. And, you know, and I don't remember how I found. That's the part where I wish I could find the daughter. I don't know they would tell me how I found them, but maybe they could add something to that part which I wish I could. Because I would really help them today. I'm in a position to. If they need help, I could help them. Because I feel I owe them a gratitude and some debt. The fact that they didn't chase me out of their house. And it was bitter cold. They had a fire, it was warm. And sometimes I would warm myself there many a day or a night. And they helped me the most from anybody. They were the poorest, but they helped me the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever try to go to anybody else's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was. They had a few people that helped me. It took about four people. And if it was an hour or two, each person helped me. But that hour, there was two things. The fact that a person showed you human kindness was something. It made you feel good. If somebody gave you a glass of milk, that was in itself a big [YIDDISH], a kindness. Anyway a person showed you some human feeling, kindness, this was a big help in survival. Because if I didn't have those few people that helped me one way a little bit, whether it was an hour or two, that helped. I had a few people that I knew that I could-- they would not give me away. That in itself was another kindness. If a person didn't give you away, if you didn't have to be afraid like a little animal something. I knew that alone helped me to have the courage to go on. You see, that gave me courage. And so I don't remember how I found those four different people, but I did. And during the summer it was easier because you could walk out in the summer. The sun was shining. The summers in Poland, they're nice. They're not too hard. And when the summer came I could pick berries or mushrooms. I learned which mushrooms to pick in the woods. They showed me, those two daughters. And the mushrooms grew right near their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. That was Juska-- Julia and Juska. And they all had with a J I think the first names. The boy's name was Juzek. And they all had with a J. And I believe their last name was Koryszina. I think so, believe so. But, you know, I didn't remember the name till I went back to Poland and I went to visit that house. I tried to find the house. And the people who lived there now told me I think their name was Koryszina. I believe so. And I wrote it down, but I didn't prepare to give you. But I believe it Koryszina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the children nice to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't-- yeah, they weren't mean to me. It's amazing. I can't believe it. I think it was amazing that they were so tolerant of me, because I was very quiet. I was very, I didn't-- there wasn't-- you know, demand-- I didn't demand anything from them. I didn't even ask for food. If they didn't give me I just sat quiet alone. I only waited till they gave me, you understand? I was so good that you wouldn't believe it how good. In other words, I tried to behave myself, behave so that I shouldn't annoy them. And, oh, and they were very compassionate I think. You know, to be compassionate in those days, you cannot imagine what it meant. Because nobody was compassionate. The very few people, very few people. Let me correct. Very few people from the whole village were compassionate, you could rely. And they were compassionate. It means like something out of the ordinary. To find a person who-- Because, you see, they claimed that they could have been killed if somebody would have told on them, which is true. If somebody saw me and they decided to report that I'm there, the German would come with a gun and kill me like a dog and they would have been also punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see? But, on the other hand, if somebody gave away and said they are hiding there they got a prize. Every Jewish person had a price on their head. I don't remember what the price was. I remember they told me, but I forgot.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a price on every child, every person to the German. The Pole who gave away to the German had a price, was given a price. Every Jewish soul, person, life was worth to them a price. And there were such people in that village who did just that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so the people who didn't do especially that, but you still had to be afraid-- every person was my potential enemy, because I didn't know who was going to give me away or who not. But I found a few people who I knew that were not going to give me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those were the righteous Gentiles. Those were the ones who deserved to be praised. You understand? To be acknowledged that there were such a people who took the chance and gave me a hiding for the amount of time they gave me. And I'm forever thankful and I will always remember. I'm sure that when they die and they come before the Ribbono Shel Olam they'll be rewarded. I'm sure of that. Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One lady who was an older lady, I found her in the village which was the next village where the train arrived. It was called Chalupki. She was Lady Gdynska. This lady Gdynska was an older lady. And she also was a kind lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd never really slept in her house itself, but I slept in her barn in the place where she kept the one-- I remember I let myself in and I knew she won't report me. And I slept near the cow in the place where the cows and the horse slept. And I remember it was nice and warm because they gave off such a warmth. But I was afraid, are they going to stamp on me? But they are really actually in a separate, they each one is in a separate little, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, I slept there one night in her barn where she kept the hay and all this. So in the morning if she would come in and she'd find me she would bring me a glass of milk. You see, her daughter-in-law lived in the same house with her only son. And she was-- her daughter-in-law was afraid for her life, because she was born Jewish. So she was always afraid she's going to be suspect. So she was, if she knew that I'm in the house she would be afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her mother-in-law was afraid to let me in the house. Only when she was out she would let me in, you know, into the house. And she would try to be kind to me and talk to me. She would make me feel like a human being, like a child, like a human child.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words-- but she was very, very religious. And she was so religious that today if you come to that village and you ask for that name they would tell you [POLISH]. It means the holy Lady Gdynska. She's known as the Holy Lady Gdynska. That was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she had one son. She was not poor. She had a horse and a cow in the barn. And she had the pigs, what do you call them? Pigs, I think. She had several, I think, the pigs and things. So that was considered well off, you know, by the amount, by the animals you had. And she had a barn. And she was not as poor as the other lady who helped me more. But she was kind and she was a righteous Gentile.

speaker: interviewee
text: She would be considered a righteous Gentile. Because she didn't hide me, but she didn't give me away. And she showed me human kindness in the war when nobody wanted to show human kindness.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she used to say to you that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: --made you feel that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She just made me feel good. She just didn't throw me out. Get out of my house! You are Jewish. And she introduced me to some other people. I used to come to their house and sit down sometimes, a few of her friends. And I remember I was sitting in one of her friend's house and then I remember seeing plants. And it made me feel good looking at the plants. And I realized there's some life out there, that not everybody is hiding. And I see that it was a nice, warm house and I felt good for the temporarily. And they let me stay there. And I remember one day another friend came. And they wanted to take me to I should help in their house and they'll keep me. So I started to walk out of the house, like one connected me to the other. And then this lady who wanted to take me to her house happened to live in the very center of the town where the Jewish people once lived. And I knew I would have been recognized, one way or another. And I actually started to walk with her on the road going to her home. On the way I suddenly woke up and I realized, my gosh. If I go where she told me she's living, I'm going to be killed. I said, no thank you. Not so well, I said, I changed my mind. Because I realized what I have to do for me is what my brain tells me to do. The brain that I had at that-- in other words, the seykhl that I had at that time, the common sense, whatever I had, told me that I shouldn't do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I listened to my common sense. Whether it was the smartest or not, I don't know. All I know is that my common sense told me, don't do this. Because I was afraid. If my common sense said this, you don't do, you don't do. That's it. I used my first common sense that came into my head. And I realized going on that road that on one hand I could have maybe a sort of-- I could have a bed and place and some food, but I didn't want to be killed. I didn't want to take the chance. Because I made up my mind, I'm going to survive at all odds. And I wasn't going to do anything that's comfortable at the moment, but I wasn't going to be killed. And nobody's going to have a chance to find out who I am to be killed, OK? So I went back. And I decided to take my chances the way I survived. And I walked back. I remember that day very clearly. And I didn't take her offer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a few offers like this, but I didn't. I was afraid. One way or the other, somebody-- I was always afraid that if I stay one place, somehow the Germans will get a hold of that I am hiding. And I didn't want to give them that chance. I decided I'll be one step ahead of them. In other words, I figured a way that I will survive this way, not-- you know, not by hiding or having a comfortable home and then be caught or whatever. So that's how I survived. And this Lady Gdynska was kind. But in her way she was so religious. She thought-- she was taught that you save a soul, a Jewish soul, by trying to either to baptize me or to make me into a Catholic like her. So she did have her reason for wanting me to become nun. In other words, she wanted-- each time I came into her house she was nice to me, but she also took some water from-- there was on the wall like a container. I don't know if this water was from the church or they made holy, whatever. And she tried to sprinkle me, like, you know, and to bap-- not bap-- One day she said, you know, after she knew me awhile she said, you know, I'll take you to the priest and he'll baptize you and he'll make you into a real Catholic little child. And this part I blocked out of my mind for 40 years. I didn't remember it till I went back to Poland and I met this Jewish woman who was born a Jewish woman, but she is a Catholic. She practices the Catholic religion. She was born into a Jewish family, and I found her. And she reminded me something that I'd blocked out. And she told me about me what lady, the old Lady Gdynska told her. And that part I didn't remember till I went back to Poland. And she told her after I left Grodzisko they were talking about me, how smart I was to survive, how I managed to do all this. When the war ended they knew that I made it. I don't know how, but they knew I made it. And they didn't understand how I made it. That I was so bright that I did it against all odds. And she reminded, this lady, this Jewish Polish woman. She told her that I one day I came and she wanted to baptize me. And I forgot about this. She wanted to take me to the priest and baptize me. And I forgot about this. And she told her, to this Jewish Polish woman, that one day when I came and she said she's going to take me to the priest and going to baptize me, I went like this with my hands, like this. And I said, please, please not. She even showed me how I looked, how I made, how my body went to sort of like into a very-- into a position where I felt like I was at her-- I was at her mercy. But I didn't want to insult her and I didn't know how to handle the situation. So I sort of went like this. And I said, please, not yet, not yet, I want to wait a little longer. She showed me how I looked, how I did that. And had this old lady showed what I did and how I said it. And she reminded me after forty years and I completely forgot this. And she also told me that this lady Gdynska put a big cross on me. Here. And I had this cross, that's what she said. That I had this cross and she named me. She told me that I should say my name is Marisha and that this is my name. That I should say that's my name. And I didn't have a second name. I was so unprepared for being hidden or anything that if somebody questioned me I would have been dead. Because I didn't have even my second name prepared. I was so unprepared. I was just-- I was lucky that nobody ever arrested me, nobody ever questioned me officially. I was just-- I believe that-- the way I see it now that there must have been a maluch on this side and a maluch on this side. It's very possible. And those maluchem were guiding me and protecting me from that nothing bad should happen. That's the only way I can explain it. Because there was no way I could have been that smart that I could've avoided the Germans I saw in the street and Poles that saw me and some of them knew I was Jewish and didn't give me away. All this, there was-- it's impossible this should have happened with all this my doing. I believe there was a maluch, either one or two, and guiding me and protecting me that I should survive. And that's the only way I can explain it. That's it. What I did, I did. But to have that much protection that nothing should have happened to me against all odds. I walked. I was visible, and nobody ever arrested me. And I can tell you incident that there is no way that I could have been not protected about without supernatural help. There's no way. I cannot forget that this could have happened any other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that there was an incident. What incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thought-- should I mention that incident? OK. One day-- you see, one day there was-- it happened that not far where I was in the edge of the woods by this lady with the two daughters that one day the Nazis-- the Nazi, because there was only one. He came and somebody reported there were Jewish hiding, Jewish people hiding in this house where it was like all alone in the woods, almost like the edge of the woods. It was actually in the woods, not deep in the woods, but there was woods surrounding. And she was very primitive. The house had a thatched roof and there was a log cabin there. And 10 years ago when I went back the house was still standing and didn't burn down. And the lady is still living 10 years ago just at the primitive way the way 50 years ago. And this lady, I found out, she hid some Jewish people. And I found it out later. I didn't know it that they were hiding there. I came, used to come into the house. I used to sit. I used to watch how they worked outside, you know, threshing the wheat or whatever. And I was just watching all this, taking it in. Gave me something to do. And they wouldn't chase me out. They knew I was Jewish. And they had children. They didn't tell about. They didn't say anything that I was-- you know, tell on me. They didn't tell other children. They were kind. There was a kind lady. And I didn't know that upstairs they were hiding Jewish people. In fact, one day I think she came and gave me a piece of material from somebody, I didn't know who gave it to me. It was a piece of silk, green silk. The Jewish people who were hiding gave her some silk to give to me. I didn't know what I was going to do with this silk. I gave it to somebody that knew how to sew. I gave it to them. I didn't know what to do with it. I didn't ask them to make nothing from me. I just gave it to her. I knew she was a dressmaker. I gave it to her and that was it. See that's how, I didn't even use out my full-- I didn't take advantage of every-- you know, if I did have something. I was so, you know, very innocent. Very-- [SIGHS] Anyway, I didn't know how to even speak up for myself. I just needed a very basic minimum. Whatever I needed, I took. In other words, a piece of bread I needed I took, I asked that's it. Milk, whatever, if someone want to give me, fine. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these people were hiding, I found out, upstairs, and I didn't know it. I was in their house I didn't know they were upstairs. And one day somebody must have-- who gave them away, I don't know. And they were literally-- they came, the Nazi and with a gun and shot some of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: One ran away. He managed to escape. And he did reach American soil eventually, one man. And the others ran out and they were shooting and they shot them. And this woman who were hiding the Jewish, they didn't do nothing to them. But after the war somebody came, maybe Poles, someone. They shot the husband, shot her husband because they were hiding Jewish people. This was after the war. She told me. When I came to visit her 10 years ago she told this story. That her husband was killed, she thinks because they were hiding Jewish people. This was after the war. Now, when I heard this-- I was by this other lady, not far within the distance of a block when this happened. Their young daughters came and said they're shooting. They heard that they shot some people and I must leave. And so I left. And I didn't know. And this was in the evening and it was getting dark. It was dark was setting in early.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it winter? Summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter. I was just coming to that. There was snow was very high then. And I remember it just like today as if it happened today. I can still see that snow. And I started to walk not with the fields like I usually walked because I didn't have shoes to protect me from the snow. I knew I will walk into the snow I'm going to get wet. So I walked with the main road.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was dangerous, because the main road the Germans patrolled sometimes or somebody could have seen me. Everybody would have known who would walk, a crazy person, in the middle of winter in the night? They would have known that I was Jewish. And they would have gone to the city and reported me and I would've been dead. Shot like a dog. So that's why I say I believe there was a maluch walking near me and protected me and walked with me on that road. Because how does a child walk in the middle and nobody saw me, nobody discovered me on the main road of Grodzisko? And then I had to turn on the main road to Chalupki. That's where my destination was, to that other lady, Gdynska. And I was going there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they told me to leave I left because I knew what they were saying. I realized that this was-- they're going to be shot or punished so I understand so I had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I left.
